text: Natural language processing helps computers understand, analyze, and generate human language.
Tokenization splits text into smaller units such as words, subwords, or sentences.
Lowercasing text can reduce noise when case does not carry important meaning.
Stopword removal can simplify text, but it should be used carefully in tasks like sentiment or toxicity detection.
Stemming reduces words to rough roots, while lemmatization uses dictionary forms.
Lemmatization often keeps words more readable than stemming.
Punctuation may carry meaning in tasks such as sarcasm detection or chat moderation.
Text normalization makes messy text easier to process before modeling.
A bag of words representation counts word presence without considering order.
TF-IDF gives higher importance to words that are frequent in a document but rare across the corpus.
TF-IDF is a strong baseline for many text classification tasks.
Sparse vectors from TF-IDF can work very well with linear models.
Logistic regression predicts the probability of a class using a sigmoid function.
Linear SVM finds a separating boundary that can work well for high-dimensional text data.
Precision tells you how often predicted positives are actually correct.
Recall tells you how many actual positives your model successfully found.
F1 score balances precision and recall into one useful metric.
Accuracy can be misleading when the dataset is imbalanced.
Class imbalance is common in toxicity, spam, and fraud detection datasets.
A confusion matrix shows true positives, false positives, true negatives, and false negatives.
Error analysis helps you understand where a model fails and why.
Saying a model is good is not enough; you should inspect its mistakes.
N-grams capture short word sequences and help preserve local context.
Character n-grams can help in noisy text with spelling variations.
Subword tokenization helps modern transformer models handle rare words.
Word embeddings represent words as dense vectors that capture meaning.
Word2Vec learns embeddings from surrounding context.
GloVe learns word vectors from global co-occurrence statistics.
FastText represents words using character n-grams, which helps with rare words and morphology.
Classic word embeddings are useful, but they do not fully capture sentence meaning by themselves.
Context matters because the same word can mean different things in different sentences.
BERT reads both left and right context to build contextual embeddings.
BERT is strong for understanding text rather than directly generating it.
Sentence-BERT adapts BERT for sentence similarity and semantic search.
Sentence embeddings turn whole sentences into vectors that reflect meaning.
MiniLM is a lightweight transformer model that produces efficient sentence embeddings.
MiniLM is popular because it is fast, compact, and effective for retrieval tasks.
Semantic search retrieves text based on meaning rather than exact word overlap.
Cosine similarity measures how close two vectors are in direction.
High cosine similarity usually means two texts are semantically related.
A query about Ronaldo should retrieve football-related documents even if the exact word football is not present.
Embedding models can connect concepts that share meaning without sharing the same words.
This is why semantic search feels smarter than keyword search.
RAG stands for Retrieval-Augmented Generation.
RAG combines retrieval of relevant documents with generation of a final answer.
A retriever finds useful chunks before the language model writes the response.
RAG helps reduce hallucination by grounding answers in source documents.
A vector database stores embeddings so similar items can be found quickly.
FAISS is a fast similarity search library for vectors.
ChromaDB is a convenient vector store that can save embeddings, text, and metadata.
FAISS is great for fast vector search, while ChromaDB is easier for end-to-end RAG workflows.
Metadata helps you track where a retrieved chunk came from.
Chunking splits long documents into smaller pieces that are easier to embed and retrieve.
Chunk overlap helps preserve context across neighboring text segments.
PDF loading converts document files into readable text for downstream NLP pipelines.
Legal, academic, and technical PDFs are common sources for RAG systems.
A good chunk should be small enough to retrieve precisely but large enough to keep meaning.
Pretrained models save time because they already learned useful language patterns.
Fine-tuning adapts a pretrained model to your specific task or domain.
Prompting can guide a large language model without changing its weights.
Zero-shot prompting asks a model to solve a task without task-specific training.
Few-shot prompting gives the model a few examples before the actual input.
Instruction tuning teaches models to follow human-style directions more reliably.
Large language models can generate fluent answers, summarize text, and transform style.
An LLM is not just a text generator; it is also a pattern learner and reasoning helper.
T5 treats almost every NLP task as a text-to-text problem.
FLAN-T5 is a more instruction-following version of T5.
FLAN-T5 can be used for question answering, summarization, translation, and generation.
LLM-based systems become stronger when they receive relevant retrieved context.
A retrieval chain connects query understanding, document search, and response generation.
LangChain helps wire together components like loaders, splitters, retrievers, and LLMs.
LangChain reduces boilerplate when building retrieval-based applications.
A document loader reads raw files and prepares them for processing.
A text splitter breaks long input into manageable chunks.
An embedding function converts text into vectors.
A retriever returns the most relevant chunks for a query.
A generation model turns retrieved evidence into a natural answer.
A well-designed RAG system should be both accurate and explainable.
Response quality improves when retrieval is precise and the source context is trustworthy.
Evaluation for RAG should consider answer relevance, faithfulness, context precision, and context recall.
Faithfulness checks whether the answer stays grounded in the retrieved context.
Answer relevance checks whether the response actually addresses the user question.
Context recall checks whether important supporting information was retrieved.
Context precision checks whether the retrieved context was useful rather than noisy.
RAGAS provides metrics for evaluating RAG systems.
Good evaluation helps you see whether the problem is retrieval, generation, or both.
A semantic search engine is a great beginner project for learning modern NLP.
A toxicity classifier is a strong classical NLP project for learning text features and evaluation.
Comparing Logistic Regression and SVM teaches you how model choice affects results.
Embedding-based retrieval is a natural next step after classical text classification.
A personal knowledge base can turn your notes into a searchable assistant.
A domain-specific chatbot works best when its knowledge comes from trusted documents.
The best NLP projects often combine simple components in a clever way.
You do not always need to train a model from scratch to build something useful.
Using pretrained models can help you focus on the application instead of the heavy training.
Modern NLP is often about building systems, not just building models.
Retrieval is the memory layer of many AI assistants.
Generation is the writing layer of many AI assistants.
Semantic similarity is the bridge between text understanding and retrieval.
RAG is powerful because it lets the model search first and answer second.
